And so — I worked. There are lots of jobs on a hive world like Sigius VI. None of them are clean and none of them pay well, but I learned self-discipline, as an alpha should. Supporting only myself, I was able to set credits aside, and train. I fell in love with the huntress traditions of old Hauâ Mai — before my kind became soft and decadent. The Nua, who lived the lives alphas were meant to. I don’t know what the human equivalents of those would be.” [silly |“Whilst they were partying, you were studying the blade,” you murmur. |“Knights, or samurai perhaps,” you murmur. ] “Yes, that sounds about right,” the dzaan nods. “Those huntresses of old... they combined the erotic with the martial. Prowess on the field, prowess in the bedroom, where you’d take your enemies after you defeated them, make them moan as you pounded them like you pounded them in battle, their bellies soon to be swollen with your children. Prowess that speaks of your alpha-hood in a way words could never. It’s a drumbeat, a rhythm, pulsing out through all the galaxy. You’re either the stick... or the skin.” She sighs, doting on you in a decidedly maternal fashion lost in a happy daze. “It was easy for me to find work with the PMCs,” she says at last. “By the time I was old enough to qualify, I could shoot, I could bandage, I could disarm with my fists and feet. But more than that, the merc groups like contractors who can disable targets by sexual means: less messy, softens criminals up for their transitioning into whores, menial workers and dolls for the market. And I could do that. Eventually, I wound up with SagiTom. They accept only the best — to work for them you must prove you are cold and steady of heart, eye and mind. They pay the best, that’s what mattered to me.” You murmur something to the effect that she never struck you as someone motivated by money. “It’s not for me.” Jasvalla pauses, violet eyes elsewhere. “I make sure father gets the credits discreetly, and not every month. It would humiliate her to be supported by me. I just want her to know her daughter — that she made it out. That she became a success.” The dzaan stumbles a little at the end there, a rare enough phenomenon for it to stand out like a flare rising from endless, bare brown hills.

Her mellow tones are still as utterly assured as they were the day she accosted you in that elevator. “Do you think lawmen, judges, prisons can keep me from you, can deny fate? I haven’t taught you well enough, beta-to-be. But I will! I will!” “Man,” mutters the kalabast standing next to you. “And I thought my ex was bad.” You are finally rid of your dzaan stalker, a huge relief. Maybe one day you’ll be able to reacquaint yourself with her, on your terms this time, somewhere else.
